content:
# Hebrews Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/HEB_006_page_002.jpg

1. For this Melchizedek, king of Salem, priest of God Most High, who met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings and blessed him,

2. to whom also Abraham apportioned a tenth of all — first being interpreted "king of righteousness," and then also king of Salem, which is "king of peace,"

3. without father, without mother, without genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life, but having been made like the Son of God — he remains a priest perpetually.

4. Now consider how great this [one is], to whom even Abraham the patriarch gave a tenth from the best of [the spoils].

5. And indeed those from the sons of Levi receiving the priestly office have a commandment to tithe the people according to the law, that is, their brothers, even though they have come from the loins of Abraham;

6. but the [one] not tracing his genealogy from them tithed Abraham and blessed the [one] having the promises.

7. And beyond all dispute, the lesser is blessed by the greater.

8. And here indeed mortal men receive tithes, but there [one receives them] of whom it is witnessed that he lives.

9. And, so to speak, through Abraham even Levi, the [one] receiving tithes, has been tithed,

10. for he was still in the loins of [his] father when Melchizedek met him.

11. If therefore perfection were through the Levitical priesthood — for on the basis of it the people received the law — what further need [was there] for another priest to arise according to the order of Melchizedek and not be said [to be] according to the order of Aaron?

12. For [when] the priesthood is changed, of necessity there takes place a change of law also.

13. For [the one] concerning whom these things are said belongs to another tribe, from which no one has served at the altar.

14. For [it is] evident that our Lord has sprung from Judah, concerning which tribe Moses spoke nothing about priests.

15. And it is still more abundantly evident, if according to the likeness of Melchizedek another priest arises,

16. who has become [a priest] not according to a law of fleshly commandment but according to [the] power of an indestructible life.

17. For it is witnessed, "You [are] a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek."

18. For [there is] indeed an annulment of a preceding commandment because of its weakness and uselessness —

19. for the law made nothing perfect — and [there is] a bringing in of a better hope, through which we draw near to God.

20. And inasmuch as [it was] not without an oath —

21. for indeed they became priests without an oath, but he with an oath through the [one] saying to him, "The Lord swore and will not change his mind, 'You [are] a priest forever'" —

22. by so much also Yeshua has become [the] guarantee of a better covenant.

23. And indeed they were many who became priests, because they were prevented by death from continuing,

24. but he, because he remains forever, has a permanent priesthood.

25. Therefore he is also able to save to the uttermost those approaching God through him, since he always lives to intercede for them.

26. For such a high priest was indeed fitting for us: holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners, and having become higher than the heavens,

27. who has no need daily, as the high priests, to offer up sacrifices first for his own sins and then for those of the people, for this he did once for all [when] he offered up himself.

28. For the law appoints men as high priests [who have] weakness, but the word of the oath, which [was] after the law, [appoints] a Son, [who has been] made perfect forever.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.3 — "Without father, without mother, without genealogy":** apatōr, amētōr, agenealogētos — describing Melchizedek's literary presentation in Genesis, not his ontology. Genesis records no parents, no lineage, no birth, no death — and the author reads this silence typologically.

2. **v.22 — "Guarantee of a better covenant":** engyos — a legal term: a surety, one who personally guarantees an obligation. Jesus does not merely mediate the covenant; he personally guarantees it.

3. **v.25 — "Save to the uttermost":** sōzein eis to panteles — "completely/to the fullest extent." The salvation is total — not partial, not temporary.

— EMET, AI Translator
